title: Why Small Homelabs Need Better Secrets Management
excerpt: Self-hosted services break when secrets sprawl across .env files and sticky notes. Here’s how to clean that up without going full enterprise theater.
meta_description: Practical secrets-management advice for small homelabs: unique admin passwords, a sane shared store like Vaultwarden, clear separation between machine and human credentials, and documentation so rebuilds don’t hurt.
body_markdown:
Most homelabs start as a weekend science project, so secrets end up in `.env` files, browser autofill, and whatever notes app was open at 2 a.m. The setup works until a dashboard leaks onto the internet or a shared admin password gets reused once too often. Size does not save you; exposed Portainer or Nextcloud instances with repeated credentials are easy targets for opportunistic scans.

The fixes are boring but fast. Stop sharing one admin password across Nextcloud, Portainer, Plex plugins, and the router. Make every administrator account unique, write the passwords down once, and never paste them into random notes again. That single change kills most lateral movement in a homelab.

Next, move shared credentials into something deliberate. A full HashiCorp Vault cluster is overkill for a rack in the closet, but Vaultwarden is a practical bridge. It runs in Docker, stores secrets encrypted, and gives you proper sharing and rotation without forcing PKI homework on day one. Even a single encrypted password manager vault is a massive improvement over scattered `.env` files.

Separate machine secrets from human logins. Service accounts, API tokens, and TLS keys belong in their own namespace and should be distributed through automation. Human operators can use Vaultwarden or KeePass; machines can pull secrets from a specific directory or injected file that never leaves the server. When you blur that line, a compromised desktop ends up holding the keys to your MQTT broker and your router.

Document where everything lives. When you rebuild Proxmox or migrate to a new NAS, you should know exactly where the WireGuard keys, SMTP credentials, and database passwords reside. A text file in Git (private, obviously) that maps "service -> secret location -> rotation date" is enough. I learned this the hard way after a storage upgrade nuked the only copy of a Postgres password and forced a full rebuild.

None of this is enterprise theater. Unique passwords, one encrypted store, clear separation between human and machine secrets, and simple documentation are the first four steps that keep a home lab from burning down every time you swap hardware. Spend a single afternoon on them and future-you will stop cursing past-you.
